narrative:
As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, LSAT score, family background, and other details to forecast your bar performance. For you specifically, it calculated a 90.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is an extremely high risk—far above what the school considers acceptable for admission. This led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to succeed on the bar.

The three biggest factors driving this prediction, in order of influence, were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your LSAT score. Your Year 3 decile of 1—meaning you ranked in the bottom 10% of your school that year—had the strongest pull toward the failure prediction, as it signals to the model a significant struggle with advanced coursework compared to typical students who average around the middle of their class. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1 reinforced this concern, pushing the prediction further toward failure because early poor performance often predicts ongoing challenges, unlike the average student's mid-range start.

Your LSAT score of 27 also contributed toward the failure outlook, standing well below the dataset average of about 37, which the model sees as a key barrier for bar success. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 aligns closely with the typical 3.21 average and your middle family income bracket is unremarkable, and being a full-time student matches the norm for 93% of applicants, these didn't offset the top concerns. In contrast to many admitted students who boast higher deciles and LSATs in the 30s or above, your profile clusters with those historically at high risk of bar failure.

To move forward, focus on strengthening these weak areas: retake the LSAT aiming for at least the mid-30s, build a stronger academic record through postgraduate courses or a master's program to boost your decile equivalents, and gather letters of recommendation highlighting your resilience as an Asian male overcoming early hurdles. Consider community college or targeted prep programs that have helped similar students improve bar passage rates. Reapply after demonstrating clear progress—the model rewards evidence of growth, and many in your position have turned rejections into acceptances this way.
